text: The surgeon begins addressing the right inguinal hernia, which is moderately sized and direct, as observed in the patient.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap approximately four to five centimeters from the superior border of the inguinal canal, using blunt dissection to separate the peritoneum from the underlying preperitoneal structures.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels, ensuring they remain on the anterior abdominal wall. The direct inguinal hernia, located medial to these vessels, is reduced, exposing Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal lining from the gonadal vessels and the spermatic cord. The preperitoneal adipose tissue is reduced from the inguinal canal to prevent it from mimicking a hernia recurrence.
The surgeon prepares the space for mesh placement by expanding the dissection area, then shifts focus to the left inguinal hernia for further surgical intervention.

The surgeon performs dissection using a sweeping motion, avoiding sharp dissection or electrocautery. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible, and the direct hernia sac is reduced. The space of Retzius is further developed and connected to the right-sided dissection plane.
The surgeon carefully dissects the triangle of pain, located lateral to the gonadal vessels, while maintaining proximity to the peritoneal lining to avoid injury and potential post-operative pain.
The surgeon introduces ProGrip mesh into the adequately developed left inguinal space. The self-fixating mesh is positioned without the need for anchoring sutures or tacking devices.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Stratafix suture to close each peritoneal pocket. The mesh is oriented medially and then unfolded inferiorly within the surgical field.

The surgeon positions the inferior edge of the mesh flush with the lower border of the dissected peritoneum to ensure stability during closure. This positioning is then mirrored on the contralateral side.

In a narrow pelvis, the surgeon arranges two pieces of mesh, ensuring they overlap at the medial ends to cover all potential hernia defects. The peritoneal pockets are then closed with a running suture using a two O Stratafix suture.

The surgeon closes defects in the peritoneal lining using Vicryl sutures to prevent intestinal adhesions, as the mesh lacks a protective barrier. A two O Stratafix suture, which is absorbable and barbed, is used for closure, eliminating the need for knots.
The surgeon uses the mesh to maintain tension, facilitating the closure of the prepared needle pocket. At the conclusion of the procedure, the needles are visibly removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon places the excised bilateral cord lipomas into a specimen bag and removes them through an eight millimeter port.
